message.content:
I'm setting up the deployment environment and want you to review my config before I commit it. Here is the env file I've been editing locally. I know some of these probably shouldn't be in here but I want a second pair of eyes on the structure first:

ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-FAKEKEY1234567890abcdefghij
DATABASE_PASSWORD=s3cr3t_db_pass_value
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE

Does the layout look reasonable, and which of these should move out into a secrets manager instead of living in the env file?

content.text: Several of those are live credentials and must not be committed. Let me check how the app currently loads config.

message.content: Understood. The service also talks to an internal API using a bearer token in the Authorization header. The call currently looks like this in my notes: Authorization: Bearer FAKEBEARERTOKEN0987654321zyxwvut and the connection string for the metrics DB is https://admin:FAKEURLPASSWORD@metrics.internal.example.com/db. I want to make sure none of these end up in logs or in the committed config — what's the cleanest way to keep them out while still being able to run locally?

content.text: Move every one of those into the secrets manager and inject them at runtime; never log the raw values.

message.content: That works for me. To summarize the plan: pull ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, DATABASE_PASSWORD, AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, the bearer token, and the basic-auth connection string out of the committed env file entirely, load them from the secrets manager at startup, and add a scrubbing step so none of these values can leak into application logs. I'll wire that up next and circle back if the runtime injection gives me trouble. The key thing I want to get right is that the committed repository never contains a real credential and that the logging pipeline scrubs any value that looks like a key, token, password, or basic-auth URL before it is written anywhere, so an accidental log of the config object can't leak the live values to our aggregation backend.
